speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 19, 1996. We are with Cela Sarna. This is tape five. Mrs. Sarna, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] have you thought about, over the years, what it was that might have caused you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I wasn't any better than any other of my sisters or any other people where they really didn't live to see the liberation. I don't know. Maybe it was luck, or maybe it was meant to be. I really can't answer this question. Because when it comes to being good, my mother was called [NON-ENGLISH]. I cannot say it in English. That means she was always good. She used to come Friday. She used to go to the homes and take some bread and take some challah and take some fish and take it to the people where they didn't have it. So I know that she was much better than I was. Why I survived, I cannot answer that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about your experiences in the Holocaust very often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite often. I think if we get together three people, what they lived through, that's the only subject we talk about is the camps, how we survived. And sometimes we think about it. Sometimes I think about it. Was it really me who went through that? How could I survive? Is it really possible that I went through that? Because it's so many else already, and you think about it. But whatever I told you was 10 times worse, or 10 times more, because we had to live five years in agony in three hours or four hours. So it's really couldn't not describe this in such a short time. We just took out things, what I still remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your daughter about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's why she was so worried. I never talked about it because whenever I opened my mouth, I started crying, so this was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to tell your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was really making me, my daughter. She talked about it. We told her about it. She bought me the pill what I took. And I decided to do it. I figured, whatever I can, I'll tell. But I was pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Better than I thought I will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have there been any decisions you've made in your life, or the way you've lived your life, that were affected by your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of decisions. I cannot even-- I don't go to movies. I do not go to movies because they-- unless it's a musical. I cannot see-- I never watch religious movies, even-- even on television, because they persecute people, mostly in religious movies. And in the movies, they beat up each other, they shoot, and I get so upset that everything comes from my-- before my eyes. I do not go to movies. Very seldom. I do go to musicals. I go to ballets. I go tomorrow too. I go to a-- but not to movies, because usually the movies are very frightening for me, they're very violent to me, and I cannot see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think that we will bring your family in now for the family picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was an experience even to go through this, but I went through it better than I thought I will.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, Mrs. Sarna, we would like you to introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my husband. We just celebrated 50 years, and he's just the most wonderful husband that anybody can ask for. And this is my daughter. This is the only child I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Shirley Sarna Nelson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she calls herself Sarna.

speaker: interviewee
text: She is Sarna. It's an ongoing conversation in our home. I'm Sarna to everybody in the world except my mother, father and the doorman.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. And she is the most wonderful child, too. I couldn't ask for a better child than I have. And this is my grandson, and this is the pride of our life. I have another one, which didn't come, but he is just one of a million. He comes every Saturday, almost, when he's home, to be with my husband to go to shul. He goes to Harvard, and he is doing very, very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Now you can talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, that's true. As we've celebrated holidays and family get-togethers over the years, I think that there has been a level of poignancy in all of those that other people can't begin to understand. And I certainly wouldn't wish that, in order to get there, they would have to go through their horrific experiences that my parents did. But perhaps as a second generation person and a third generation person, we can talk about that special gift that we've been endowed with that makes the moments of togetherness, the moments of celebration, the moments of naches sweeter than you could ever imagine. As a child growing up, like everything else in the world, gifts don't come without a burden. I think that being the child of survivors-- I was born in a displaced persons camp in Germany. I have no memories of those days. My first memories are sometime in the summer of 1950, and we came to Rochester, I'm told, in February of 1950. As the child of survivors, I was always very acutely aware that I was very different. First of all, we lived in a community for most of my life when there was no one around who was even marginally similar to us. It was a very Christian community with very long roots in America, and the outlook on life was very different. So the burden of being different as a child is not a small burden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody else could talk about their grandparents, and their aunts and uncles, and their great aunts and uncles. And they could go to the doctor and the doctor could say, what's your family medical history? And they could give an answer. I was always very aware that there wasn't a long line behind me, and it made me appreciate so much more the possibilities of what was in front of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe it's that legacy that I hope that I've been able to nurture in my life, to be very appreciative of family, to be very appreciative of the opportunities to celebrate. I remember, when each of the children was born, when Eric had his bar mitzvah-- our next bar mitzvah is coming up in-- in November-- I remember very clearly the celebrations we had for my parents' 25th anniversary, their 40th anniversary, and their 50th anniversary. We, of course, thought that they were all surprises. I have to figure that my parents, who are a lot smarter than any of the rest of us, had it all figured out. But we go out of our way to make these things special because special they are, and I can't help feeling that, every time we want-- do one of these, there's a little bit of a sense-- I hope this is not censored-- there's a little bit of a sense of Hitler, up yours. You failed. We're here. We're doing it. We're living forward. We are contributing to our society. We're nurturing our heritage. We're moving forward. And that's a legacy that I think others share, but there is an element that probably can't get passed on unless you've been there. And I feel it, and I know my children feel it, and maybe that's our contribution to the next generation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I couldn't even start to talk about what-- what being part of this family-- this kind of family-- has meant to me growing up. I look at everything from a completely different point of view. And things that seem terribly important and significant and something to really get very upset over to most of my friends, for me just don't seem nearly so earth-shattering because I've grown up with-- with people who've actually had serious problems and-- and serious obstacles to overcome. And just taken from my grandparents a completely positive outlook on life. In spite of all of the negative things that have happened to them along the way, they're still people who are absolutely committed to living beautiful and joyous lives, and that's become, for each of us, the most important thing. And I don't think I could say anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I have to pick up on that because I remember, growing up as a second generation person, the Holocaust always lived in our home, but we never discussed it. I very much was aware of this extra plate at dinner every night, but I didn't feel that I was entitled to ask questions because I thought the questions and the answers were too painful. So I got my information from books. Every once in a while, my parents would say something, not because they wanted to share a story with me, but something would occur in their present lives that would resonate with what occurred in the concentration camps. And so what I would hear are-- were little narratives. Never bitter, never painful, just little stories that would pop up. And they were always very spontaneous, and they were never repeated. My husband, [? Stephen, ?] who regrettably couldn't be with us, is multi-generation American Jew and from a family that's very much part of the American fabric. For us, although we're both Jewish, it was really like an intermarriage bringing this culture and his culture together. One of the glorious things about my husband is his extraordinary optimism, and I remember-- I don't know if you remember this, Daddy, but you were once in the apartment and we were talking about it, and I said, that's the terrific thing about [? Stephen. ?] He's always so optimistic that things will work out. And my father, without skipping a beat, said, he didn't have Hitler to teach him otherwise. What is so delicious is to hear Eric talk about the strength that he's gotten and this optimistic outlook that he's gotten from this pool of suffering from which they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were really shaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know that. You are all right. You are all right. And I-- you know, I'm now a parent, and it's such a cliche, but it's a cliche because everybody says it and it's because it's true. My parents become more brilliant every day. I'm now 48. I can only think what is going to be in another 50 years. And I find it astonishing that from-- from the pieces that they had; from the concern about planning things because you never knew, if you planned them, that something horrific would come and the plan would not happen. So another person at the table was the dybbuk, somebody who could come and intervene and cut off a happiness because maybe you couldn't control that the happiness would come. From that place came, in two generations, this place that can say, this is a model for a perspective. I guess I'm much more transition. I feel that sense of optimism. I feel tremendous gratitude for being where I am and being able to do what I do. I also feel the burden, which I mentioned before.

speaker: interviewer
text: If the-- I sometimes don't feel entitled to complain about things that other people feel not only entitled to complain about but that other people should listen and feel extremely sympathetic. How can I kvetch about some minor irritation in life when I know what real trouble looks like? And it's just, this is a wonderful opportunity for all of us, and this is really a pleasure to be able to listen how this experience came this way, through here, and landed here. It's-- it's really very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say one more thing, which is that I think a lot of it definitely has to do with-- with the experience that they went through. But I think that at least 50% of it is-- is who they are individuals. Because I think it's an experience that you could leave in many different ways, and-- and I think, in our-- in our own family, one of his brothers left the Holocaust a complete atheist, basically. And others left very bitter, very pessimistic about the future, very angry at-- at what-- what has happened to them. And both of them emerged from this experience with a commitment to life with an absolute appreciation for its value. They are both people of tremendous faith to this day, and certainly any-- any that I have, I've gotten from them. And I think that the experience is definitely very important in terms of their outlook and-- and how they see life. But I think they brought a lot to it of themselves, and came out of it in an individual and very unique way.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's an important point. That's a good point, because as we've often talked about, what it was that made you the difference between being a survivor and not, a lot of it was just where you were at a particular moment. We know too many stories where the brother and sister were walking together. The sister went that way. The brother went that way. The brother didn't survive. So a lot of it was just blind circumstance.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just said it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But a lot of it also had to do with, I think, a flame that was in you that, at some point, either motivated you to, within whatever was your ability to control your circumstances-- and we know that that was very marginal-- but between the ability to be able to say, I'm not gonna get beaten by this, and the ability to say, the price of life is too high. I choose death. I often wonder what I would choose. I don't know. I really don't know that answer. I think the extraordinary thing about my parents is that there is no doubt, from anybody who knows them, that what they have is the choice of life, always. Played out in a small way. You know, if there's-- if there's there's a gathering and they're sitting around, it takes about 11 and 1/2 milliseconds before my mother and father start singing. And before long, a gathering of relative strangers are, with arms around each other, swaying to Yiddish and Hebrew music. Nobody gets carried away with the words. We use English. We use god only knows what else.

speaker: interviewee
text: We hum.

speaker: interviewee
text: We hum. Big on humming. There's that spontaneity and spark that I think enabled them to find a way to get through the horror to where they are now. And that I'm, as usual, being taught by my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the songs that you start to sing and they hum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, her song is 'Bells.' That's her song. She sings it. We-- we have a tradition where we go around the table during Yontif.

speaker: interviewee
text: Holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And holidays and Friday night dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we sing a song and everyone has to have a turn and it's become kind of the ongoing joke because my father's parents come also, and my-- my other grandfather--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he can't carry a tune!

speaker: interviewee
text: Has a reserved--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a bushel basket!

speaker: interviewee
text: Has a reserved song. He gets [INAUDIBLE] every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if you would like to demonstrate--

speaker: interviewee
text: And if he doesn't get it, all-- you know, you don't want to see the results if someone steals his song and he has to come up with another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's another thing to say--

speaker: interviewee
text: We have beautiful holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: We do. The music is always fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful. Right here in this-- we have 18, 20 people every holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And no food.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't believe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Music has always been a very important part of our lives. My mother and father are incredibly musical. I know that my mother had-- [INAUDIBLE] had experiences that she says really reduced the quality of her voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. In Majdanek, I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: If that's true, one can only imagine. Do you want to tell the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I lost-- I-- I was singing as a child. I always sing-- sang in a [INAUDIBLE] in school. And when I came to Majdanek, I lost my voice completely. I couldn't-- couldn't open my mouth. And since then, it came back, but it never came back to a normal thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's still a very beautiful voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [? OK. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we always sang, and I-- I want to say I played the violin, but I really would leave you with an impression that I played at playing the violin.

speaker: interviewee
text: She did play.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one of the things that we really enjoyed doing is that my parents would sing melodies that they grew up with. And then I would listen and scratch away with it on the violin. Oh, this is going to be a sad story, but I'm going to tell it anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at one point, when we lived in Rochester-- this is many years ago-- it was a very small and parochial community, and somehow or other, we found a books-- a music bookstore and I got a book of Yiddish songs.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still have it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was incredible because everything else I'd just-- god bless you-- been playing by ear. Well, needless to mention, what was notated on the page was much more formal than anything that they had learned. So I would start playing till they would recognize the melody, and then they would sing it the way they knew it and-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm going through and we hit a song, and suddenly I look, and there are two ashen faces next to me. The song, as I recall, was [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. And it turned out that that was a song that was sung by a woman--

speaker: interviewee
text: In camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in camp right before they thought that they were going to the extermination center. Well, now I'm this little [INAUDIBLE] scratching away on the violin. And I have these two distraught people, and I don't know what to do. All I know about that song is what you just heard. As a matter of fact, I skipped to the end and found 'Yossel, Yossel, Yossel,' which is one of those, La, la, la, la, la, la la, la, and went into fast forward, 'Yossel, Yossel, Yossel,' to get out of Glick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The memories of the music times are amoung the most warmest in my whole life, and I just remember and I wish that you were there taping for my parents 45th wedding anniversary. This was not a surprise. It was at my aunt-- aunt and uncle's house in conjunction with what has become--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was the 45th.

speaker: interviewee
text: --an annual concentration camp liberation party. And everybody-- you know, these were lifelong friends, and it was very warm and very empowering. And there was a musician who had a portable keyboard, and my mother, who was a wonderful speaker, got up to make a toast and said that she wanted to sing a song. And the song-- and I never heard the story-- the song she wanted to sing was the song that my father sang to her in lieu of giving her an engagement ring when they got married. Well, I almost died. Another story that took 43 years to hear about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you sing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe later. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: We may be cramping his style.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll have my grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: We may be cramping his style.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had a song with my grandson which we started when he was one year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we need to hear this song.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everyone-- everyone around the table has a song. My brother sang 'Twist and Shout.'

speaker: interviewee
text: That's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and-- and now--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Yiddish version.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you mention this to him, he will go red and, I mean, 12 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was this big at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not his ideal to hear that, when he was this big, he would sort of twist around the table. My grandfather has [INAUDIBLE]. She has 'Bells.' We sang [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and we-- we have a whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you sing it together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ritual every time. Well-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: A little bit. A little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here. [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how we're doing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll stand here. Let's start up.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to do it louder, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's my part. Ya da da da da.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Since one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah that was the first-- that was really-- he was a real precocious guy, and he was born in August, and September, a year later, it was Rosh Hashanah time, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We have the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. And we would--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he sang that song.

speaker: interviewee
text: He would sing that song, and he would come in with the chorus. And he also did [SINGING IN YIDDISH]. And the other grandchild had his own song. You want to tell that one? My father was-- baseball has a designated hitter. My father was the designated singer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's finish-- let's finish up here. Maybe let's finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two minutes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Two more minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to sing Adam's song.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nah, we're out of time. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, just to tell you about this, my father was the designated singer, and we would actually call when Adam was an infant. Because Adam wanted to hear the song, and we would say, Papa would you sing it? And my father would sing the song, and Adam would be happy. One day, I just, by happenstance, turned on the radio and turned on in Yiddish station, and they started singing the song. I thought my son's eyes were going to fall out of his head that now Papa's song was coming out of the radio. It was really quite wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, what can I say? I have the most wonderful family that I could ask for. I have one daughter, and I have--

speaker: interviewee
text: A wonderful son-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband, which is my son. Couldn't be better.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not ask better.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] because I have-- that's my whole life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Oliver, the dog.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Oliver, the puppy dog, who my parents babysit for and think is one of the grandchildren. If you call on vacation and say, so, how's everything? How's the dog? Mom, what's wrong? She says, oy, Oliver isn't eating. And then I know that the world has come full circle.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, all of you, for a truly wonderful time here.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]
